impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent's scope.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its '844 patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products, in violation of the Sherman Act. The court upheld the antitrust liability and corresponding damages award, finding sufficient evidence that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses beyond the patent.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of tying arrangements can still defend by showing tied products are 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses, which remains a viable defense if supported by the evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who condition licenses on exclusive purchase of unpatented products face significant antitrust exposure where those products have uses beyond the patent's scope. This reinforces that patent rights do not shield tying arrangements involving products with non-infringing applications.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct in obtaining patents related to coiled tubing and Global Tubing's Walker Process fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization. The court found genuine disputes of material fact precluding summary judgment in both directions, remanding for further proceedings including potential trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the court's refusal to grant summary judgment on Walker Process fraud claims, preserving the high evidentiary standard needed to establish that a patent was obtained through fraud for antitrust purposes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that acquire prior art during asset purchases face heightened scrutiny during patent prosecution. Failure to disclose known prior art documents, such as the CYMAX Documents here, can give rise to viable inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims that survive summary judgment.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims in a patent directed to antibody-drug conjugates with tetrapeptide linkers satisfied the written description and enablement requirements where the specification primarily disclosed dipeptide linkers.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $41 million jury verdict in favor of Seagen, holding that the asserted claims of the '039 patent were invalid for lack of written description and enablement. The specification's disclosure of dipeptide linkers was insufficient to support claims covering tetrapeptide linkers, a broader genus not adequately described or enabled. This decision significantly impacts the pharmaceutical and biotech patent landscape.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants in pharmaceutical patent litigation benefit from reinforced written description and enablement standards, providing stronger invalidity defenses against broad genus claims in the ADC and biologics space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the pharmaceutical and biotech industries face heightened risk that broad claims covering structural variants beyond what is specifically disclosed in the specification will be invalidated, potentially undermining patent portfolios covering antibody-drug conjugates and similar complex biologics.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to server-side tailoring of content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods and systems for a server to tailor content specifications to a requesting device's capabilities are patent-ineligible under § 101. The court reversed the district court's judgment for GoTV on the infringed '715 patent and entered judgment for Netflix, finding the claims directed to an abstract idea without an inventive concept. The court also reversed a finding of indefiniteness on the '865 patent claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against patent infringement suits benefit from continued robust application of § 101 to invalidate claims directed to conventional server-client content delivery processes.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to adaptive content delivery and server-side optimization of media for different devices face significant eligibility challenges, potentially devaluing portfolios in the streaming and digital media space.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to web conferencing systems with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous observation of data streams are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's dismissal for failure to state a claim, holding that the '637 patent's web conferencing claims with time-shifting capabilities were patent-ineligible under § 101. The decision reinforces that claims directed to conventional computer functionality for recording and playing back conference content lack patent eligibility.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from continued narrowing of patent-eligible subject matter for conventional software conferencing and recording functionality, reducing exposure to patent assertion entities.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering web conferencing, screen sharing, and media time-shifting technologies face increased invalidity risk under § 101.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed claim terms relating to encryption information in streaming media patents, and whether the Board's obviousness determination should be reversed based on an erroneous claim construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of DivX's patent on partially encrypted streaming media content, vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge, and remanded for further proceedings. The decision impacts the streaming technology and digital rights management patent landscape by clarifying how encryption-related claim terms should be construed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies challenging patents in IPR proceedings benefit from the Federal Circuit's willingness to correct Board claim construction errors, strengthening the IPR mechanism as a tool for invalidating improperly construed patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the streaming media and DRM space face risk that claim constructions favorable at the Board level may be reversed on appeal, potentially exposing patents to invalidation on remand.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the PTAB governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions constitute 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the instructions do not bind the Director. This preserves the PTO's broad discretion to decline IPR petitions without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO retaining broad, flexible discretion to deny IPR petitions without being constrained by formal rulemaking procedures, potentially reducing the frequency of IPR challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that rely heavily on IPR as a cost-effective mechanism to challenge asserted patents face continued risk that discretionary denial frameworks—such as Fintiv-type rules—will remain in place without the procedural safeguards of notice-and-comment rulemaking.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a party can establish priority in a trademark opposition proceeding based on common law rights acquired through an asset purchase agreement executed after the opposition was filed.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registration for the stylized 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' mark and dismissed Game Plan's opposition to UNIP's marks. UNIP acquired common law priority through a 2019 assignment that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill, even though the assignment occurred after the opposition was filed. The decision clarifies that acquired common law rights can establish priority in TTAB proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies acquiring trademark rights through asset purchases benefit from the ability to rely on acquired common law priority in TTAB proceedings, even when the acquisition occurs after an opposition is filed.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face the risk that competitors can acquire superior common law rights through post-opposition asset purchases and use them to cancel existing registrations.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development S.R.O. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly granted judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation of self-inflating tire technology, and whether correction of inventorship was properly denied.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of JMOL to Goodyear, overturning a jury verdict that had awarded Coda $2.8 million in compensatory and $61.2 million in punitive damages for trade secret misappropriation related to self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship. This decision significantly impacts how trade secret claims are evaluated in the context of collaborative technology development.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies engaged in technology collaborations benefit from the court's rigorous scrutiny of trade secret claims, affirming that JMOL remains available to overturn large jury verdicts where the evidence is insufficient to support misappropriation findings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret holders face heightened difficulty sustaining large verdicts on appeal, particularly where the claimed secrets involve technology disclosed during collaborative business relationships.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB properly applied the doctrine of foreign equivalents to refuse registration of the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops based on its meaning as a Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The court found issues with the Board's genericness and descriptiveness analysis based on the Kashmiri green tea meaning of KAHWA, including whether the Board improperly raised new grounds of refusal. This decision clarifies limits on the doctrine of foreign equivalents and the Board's authority to raise new refusal grounds on appeal.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses seeking to register foreign-language marks benefit from the court's scrutiny of the doctrine of foreign equivalents, particularly where a word has multiple meanings in different languages and the PTO's analysis relies on obscure or esoteric meanings.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether so-called 'Drag' costs in collaboration agreements with parts suppliers are properly includable in the overhead pool for government cost-plus contracts under the Cost Accounting Standards, and whether an agreement between the parties on this issue was enforceable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed cost allocation disputes in Pratt & Whitney's cost-plus government contracts, holding that an agreement between the parties regarding inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool was invalid. The court dismissed part of the appeal for lack of finality regarding the overhead allocation base but reversed the Board's finding of an enforceable agreement on Drag costs. This decision impacts defense contractors' cost accounting practices.
impacts.positiveImplications: Government contractors benefit from clarity that informal agreements with the government regarding cost allocation cannot override the requirements of the Cost Accounting Standards.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors using unconventional supplier collaboration agreements face increased scrutiny of how costs from those arrangements are allocated to government contracts, potentially reducing reimbursable overhead.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Circuit has appellate jurisdiction over a district court order imposing an $8 million bond under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act, and whether the Act is preempted by federal patent law.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed for lack of jurisdiction an appeal of a district court order that denied motions to dismiss and imposed an $8 million bond under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act against patent assertion entities. The court's jurisdictional ruling leaves in place the bond requirement and the district court's finding that Micron established a reasonable likelihood of bad faith patent assertion. The Act's validity and preemption issues remain unresolved at the appellate level.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies targeted by patent assertion entities can leverage state bad faith patent assertion statutes to impose bond requirements, creating a financial deterrent against abusive patent enforcement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face significant financial exposure from state bad faith patent assertion laws, though the Federal Circuit's jurisdictional dismissal leaves the preemption question unresolved, creating ongoing uncertainty.

impacts.caseName: Wonderland Switzerland AG v. Evenflo Company, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly upheld a jury verdict of patent infringement under the doctrine of equivalents for car seat patents and entered a permanent injunction against the infringer.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed in part and reversed in part the district court's judgment, addressing infringement under the doctrine of equivalents for car seat design patents and the scope of a permanent injunction. The court reviewed jury findings on infringement and willfulness, as well as the propriety of injunctive relief covering both asserted patents. This decision impacts product design and IP enforcement in the consumer products industry.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the consumer products space benefit from the court's willingness to uphold doctrine-of-equivalents infringement findings and permanent injunctions where supported by the evidence.
impacts.negativeImplications: Product manufacturers face risk that design-around efforts may still be found infringing under the doctrine of equivalents, and permanent injunctions may extend beyond the specific patents litigated.

impacts.caseName: Adnexus Inc. v. Meta Platforms, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court erred in dismissing a patent infringement complaint for failure to state a claim under Rule 12(b)(6) where the plaintiff attached claim charts and preliminary infringement contentions to the amended complaint.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated the district court's dismissal of Adnexus's patent infringement suit against Meta for failure to state a claim and remanded for further proceedings. The court found that the amended complaint, together with appended claim charts and infringement contentions mapping limitations onto Meta's Lead Ads product, was sufficient to survive a motion to dismiss. This decision clarifies the pleading standard for patent infringement claims.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a lower bar to survive motions to dismiss in infringement cases, as appended claim charts and infringement contentions can satisfy pleading requirements even without highly detailed factual allegations in the complaint itself.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies face increased difficulty obtaining early dismissal of patent infringement suits at the pleading stage, potentially increasing litigation costs and the need for discovery before resolving infringement disputes.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is consistent with the governing statute and whether Section 1516a abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that Section 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial proceedings and that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the statute. The court affirmed the CIT's order directing the ITC to proceed in accordance with the statute on remand. This decision significantly impacts transparency in trade remedy proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and trade litigants benefit from increased transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from public access.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting commercially sensitive information in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of public disclosure, potentially exposing proprietary business data, pricing information, and competitive intelligence in trade proceedings.
